pain: You should not have to inspect process trees or tab titles just to know whether a shell is running Claude Code, Codex, or plain bash.
Wrapper scripts, npx launches, and nested child processes make agent detection brittle in normal terminals.
subfeatures: Built-in registry for Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, ChatGPT, Copilot, Aider, Cursor, Windsurf, Cline, Cody, Amazon Q, Devin, Continue, Goose, Mentat, and Amp.
Command-line and banner matching catch agents even when they are launched through wrappers or deeper descendants.
Detection updates the tab model immediately, so branding, status badges, MCP tools, and telemetry all see the same agent identity.
No shell aliases, prompt hacks, or dotfile edits are required.

pain: When several AI sessions are open, identical tabs force you to click around just to find the right agent.
Colorless tab bars hide context that should be obvious at a glance.
subfeatures: Each supported agent gets a curated logo and brand color from the shared AI tool registry.
Tabs update live when an agent starts, changes, or exits in the same shell.
Non-AI tabs fall back to the default appearance automatically.
The same identity metadata is reused across the overlay and status views for consistent recognition.

pain: Activity Monitor shows generic node and python processes, not which AI session owns the load.
Without per-tab resource data, it is hard to kill the right runaway agent.
subfeatures: Captures the detected agent PID, CPU usage, and resident memory for each tab.
Samples are kept per tab so Claude in one session never pollutes Codex metrics in another.
Resource snapshots are exposed to tab status views and MCP tooling.
Metrics can be correlated with telemetry runs and agent activity over time.

pain: Internal CLIs and niche agents are invisible until the terminal knows how to label them.
Teams should not have to wait for an upstream release to detect their own tooling.
subfeatures: Add custom substring rules in settings for process names or launch commands.
Custom rules run alongside built-in detection instead of replacing it.
Built-in matches still win for known tools, preserving stable branding and analytics labels.
Rules live in Chau7 settings, so they can be exported or shared across a team.

pain: Checking whether an agent is still working normally costs a tab switch and a visual scan every time.
Background AI sessions are easy to forget until they finish or stall silently.
subfeatures: Shows active, idle, and completed states directly in the tab strip.
State comes from live process monitoring rather than fragile output parsing.
The badge updates without focusing the tab, so long-running sessions stay visible from the side.
Background descendants are tracked as long as they remain attached to the tab's shell tree.

pain: Running several agents at once usually turns cost, status, and ownership into a blur.
Shared state across tabs makes side-by-side AI workflows unreliable.
subfeatures: Every tab keeps its own detected agent, metrics, telemetry run, and session state.
Session listing APIs can return one tab or all active AI tabs at once.
Token, latency, and cost data remain scoped to the tab that produced them.
Detection and branding changes in one tab never leak into another.

pain: AI agents waste tokens on ANSI junk, spinner frames, and noisy command output that humans ignore instantly.
Without visibility into output hygiene, teams keep paying for context bloat they cannot see.
subfeatures: Wraps shell output with a token-optimization layer that strips escape codes, progress churn, and other terminal-only noise.
Tracks savings and runtime behavior so you can see the impact of optimization instead of guessing.
Works alongside per-tab and global control, so noisy sessions can be optimized without changing everything.
Feeds cleaner command output to AI tools while keeping the local terminal experience intact.

pain: Copying terminal errors into a browser is slow and breaks concentration.
Error text without surrounding terminal context produces worse explanations and repeated guessing.
subfeatures: One-click capture of the error plus nearby context from inside the terminal.
Provider-agnostic explanation flow supporting OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, and compatible endpoints.
Explanations render inside Chau7 instead of forcing a browser detour.
User-triggered sending only, so nothing is shipped to a provider unless you ask.

pain: A global CTO toggle is too blunt for mixed workflows.
Some tabs need clean AI-ready output while others need raw terminal fidelity.
subfeatures: Three modes per tab: default, forceOn, and forceOff.
Override is checked before the global CTO flag on every tab.
Control is available from the UI and over MCP via `tab_set_cto`.
Different tabs can run optimized and raw output side by side.

pain: Long AI runs disappear into anonymous terminal time unless the app tracks them explicitly.
It is hard to answer basic questions like how many runs happened in a tab or how long a session has been active.
subfeatures: Groups AI activity into tab-scoped sessions instead of flattening everything into one global log.
Tracks run counts, start times, and duration so live sessions are measurable.
Surfaces active session state through runtime and telemetry services.
Keeps agent identity attached to the session so downstream analytics stay attributable.

pain: AI CLIs hide API traffic behind provider SDKs, so you cannot attribute requests to a tab.
External proxies are tedious to configure and disconnected from terminal context.
subfeatures: Local proxy reroutes provider base URLs to localhost for OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini traffic.
Handles HTTPS and WebSocket flows with a local certificate and owner-only local routing.
Parses calls into structured events tied to the originating tab and session.
Feeds token, cost, and latency analytics without storing API keys in Chau7.

pain: AI bills arrive in tokens, but most terminals expose zero token visibility.
Without per-call counts, prompt tuning is just superstition.
subfeatures: Extracts input and output token counts from intercepted provider responses.
Keeps per-call, per-tab, and rolled-up totals.
Normalizes counts into a shared event model across providers.
Powers downstream cost and dashboard views.

pain: It is easy to overspend when multiple agents call different models in parallel.
Provider dashboards do not tell you which terminal tab burned the money.
subfeatures: Calculates per-call cost from provider, model, and token metadata.
Aggregates spend by tab, session, and total usage.
Stores cost alongside the underlying API event for auditing.
Lets you compare expensive sessions before they become habits.

pain: When AI feels slow, you usually cannot tell whether the bottleneck is the model, network, or workflow.
Provider dashboards are too coarse to explain one sluggish coding session.
subfeatures: Records request latency in milliseconds on every captured API call.
Keeps structured latency fields in the shared API event model.
Makes slow calls visible per tab and per provider, not just globally.
Surfaces latency alongside tokens and cost so tradeoffs are obvious.

pain: Terminal usage, AI usage, and session history usually live in separate tools if they exist at all.
Without a single dashboard, you cannot reason about command volume, success rate, and API spend together.
subfeatures: Aggregates command history, session data, and API events into one view.
Includes daily statistics and error-rate calculations from persistent history.
Surfaces totals for commands, success rate, token usage, cost, and latency trends.
Reuses the same analytics store that powers session playback and telemetry summaries.

pain: AI agents can operate terminals better when they can query and control the terminal directly.
Glue scripts around terminals are usually incomplete and brittle.
subfeatures: Exposes tab listing, status, input, execution, and session inspection as MCP tools.
Routes tool calls through TerminalControlService and related runtime services.
Returns structured results for tabs, sessions, and command output instead of screen scraping.
Shares the same per-tab model as the app UI, so agents and humans see the same state.

pain: Local automation should not require network ports or cloud services just to reach your terminal.
Stdio-only integrations make it harder to share a stable control surface across tools.
subfeatures: MCP server listens on a local Unix domain socket under the user's home directory.
Socket permissions are restricted to the same user account.
Bridge processes can pipe stdio to the socket without exposing a public port.
Each client connection gets its own MCP session handler.

pain: Local MCP servers are annoying when every tool requires hand-written config before it can connect.
Path drift across machines makes manual setup fragile.
subfeatures: Chau7 can generate or update MCP registration files automatically for supported clients.
Registration uses the local bridge and socket path so clients connect to the correct server.
The app keeps setup close to the terminal instead of asking users to maintain separate glue.
Reduces time-to-first-tool-call for Codex, Claude, and similar MCP clients.

pain: Sometimes agents need context, not just actions, and terminals rarely expose it in a structured way.
Screen scraping terminal output is a poor substitute for named resources.
subfeatures: Supports MCP resources/list and resources/read over the same local session.
Publishes named resources backed by telemetry and current terminal state.
Resource reads return structured payloads instead of raw UI text.
Resources live beside tools, so clients can discover capabilities from one server.

pain: Runaway agents can create tabs faster than you notice them.
A guardrail is only useful if it constrains automation without blocking normal human use.
subfeatures: Sets a configurable ceiling on tabs created through the MCP server.
Manual tabs are excluded, so your own workflow is never throttled.
Closed MCP-created tabs immediately free capacity for new ones.
Returns a clear error to the agent instead of silently failing.

pain: When some tabs are controllable by AI and others are just shells, ambiguity is a safety problem.
Users and agents both need a quick visual hint about which tabs are in the MCP control path.
subfeatures: Adds an MCP status marker directly to the tab UI.
Updates with live tab state instead of static configuration text.
Makes controllable contexts easy to spot inside dense multi-tab workflows.
Pairs naturally with MCP tools, limits, and approval gating.

pain: Modern terminal work spans many repos, environments, and agents; one session per window is useless.
Opening separate windows for everything destroys navigation speed.
subfeatures: Native tab model with independent shell and session state per tab.
Quick create, close, and selection flows integrated across UI and MCP tools.
Tabs cooperate with AI detection, analytics, and session tracking automatically.
Selected versus background tab behavior is explicit, enabling suspension and recovery features.

pain: Side-by-side work normally requires multiple windows or tmux complexity.
Comparing logs, editors, and shells is clumsy when one tab can show only one session.
subfeatures: Multiple panes can coexist in one workspace with independent terminal sessions.
Layout is managed by a dedicated split-pane controller for predictable resizing and focus.
Pane interactions are covered by dedicated tests, reducing layout regressions.
Lets you pair AI work, shells, and inspection panes without leaving Chau7.

pain: Static tab ordering makes active workflows harder to group or reprioritize.
Close-and-reopen is the wrong answer for simple tab organization.
subfeatures: Drag gestures reorder tabs directly in the strip.
Layout logic accounts for destination slots rather than naive swaps.
Visual movement tracks actual tab widths for smoother placement.
Reordering keeps the rest of the tab model intact, including tab identity and session state.

pain: Debugging, presentations, and remote work need different terminal settings.
Re-tuning fonts, colors, and behavior by hand for each context is wasted time.
subfeatures: Saved profiles capture appearance and behavior settings as reusable presets.
Profiles integrate with automatic switching rules for directories, SSH hosts, and other context.
Settings persist through config parsing and profile storage instead of transient UI state.
Lets teams standardize common terminal setups without rebuilding them from scratch.

pain: Inactive tabs should not keep rendering at full speed when you are not looking at them.
Naive suspension can starve the PTY and make tabs feel broken when you come back.
subfeatures: Background tabs pause expensive rendering work after a configurable delay.
The PTY still drains while suspended, so output keeps flowing even when UI updates are paused.
Tabs unsuspend immediately when selected or when AI activity makes them important.
Settings expose the suspension toggle and delay to tune CPU savings versus responsiveness.

pain: When tab rendering glitches, users should not be the recovery mechanism.
UI desync bugs are especially painful in multi-tab, AI-heavy workflows.
subfeatures: A model-owned watchdog timer checks tab bar health continuously.
It compares expected and rendered state to detect missing or stale tab UI.
Recovery uses bounded retries, cooldowns, and logging instead of infinite refresh loops.
Summary counters make watchdog interventions visible for debugging.

pain: Terminal work disappears once the scrollback moves on.
Reproducing how a bug, command, or AI run unfolded is hard without a timeline.
subfeatures: Records command and output activity into a structured session timeline.
Works with persistent history and telemetry so recordings tie back to real runs.
Provides the data model used by playback and scrubber UI.
Makes postmortems and demos possible without manual note-taking.

pain: Huge recordings are painful to scrub through using raw scroll alone.
Developers need a fast way to jump to the moment a command or failure happened.
subfeatures: Displays a visual horizontal timeline for recorded sessions.
Marks command segments so navigation is tied to meaningful activity, not just line count.
Supports scrub-style replay and quick jumping across the session.
Shares analytics and session-recording data rather than duplicating its own store.

pain: Reopening a terminal should not mean rebuilding all tabs and context from memory.
Crashes, restarts, and updates are much less costly when the workspace can come back.
subfeatures: Restores saved tab and session state after relaunch instead of starting from an empty shell set.
Preserves the multi-tab workflow context that other features build on.
Works with recorded session data and persistent history rather than transient UI only.
Reduces the cost of quitting or recovering from interruptions.

pain: CPU-bound text rendering falls apart under high-output workloads.
AI-heavy terminal sessions need smooth scrolling and low-latency redraws.
subfeatures: Uses a dedicated Metal renderer for the terminal display path.
Integrates frame pacing and low-latency view configuration for responsive updates.
Offloads heavy drawing work from the main CPU path.
Serves as the base for advanced display optimizations like triple buffering and shared surfaces.

pain: Moving rendered frames around inefficiently wastes the gains from GPU rendering.
Advanced compositing features need a better surface handoff than repeated copies.
subfeatures: Uses IOSurface-backed display coordination for frame sharing.
Keeps rendered output in a form macOS can composite efficiently.
Fits into the Metal display coordinator rather than bolting on a separate path.
Helps high-refresh terminal output remain smooth during resize and composition changes.

pain: Escape-sequence-heavy output can bottleneck before it ever reaches the renderer.
Scalar parsing wastes CPU on workloads that are embarrassingly parallel.
subfeatures: Uses SIMD-aware parsing for fast terminal byte and escape handling.
Improves throughput during bursty output such as builds, logs, and AI tool chatter.
Lives in the hot path between PTY bytes and rendered cells, where speed matters most.
Is backed by dedicated parser tests to keep correctness aligned with the speedups.

pain: Terminal responsiveness starts with input, not just rendering.
High-level input paths can add jitter or lose fidelity for advanced key handling.
subfeatures: Integrates low-level HID input plumbing for fast keyboard event handling.
Helps keep input-to-display latency low in the native macOS app.
Works alongside protocol-aware key encoding rather than replacing terminal semantics.
Supports the broader low-latency pipeline that Chau7 builds around native input and rendering.

pain: Producer and renderer contention causes stutter when output spikes.
Double-buffer style pipelines can force components to wait on each other unnecessarily.
subfeatures: Separates renderable state across three buffers to reduce pipeline stalls.
Lets parsing, preparation, and display proceed with less contention.
Smooths out bursty terminal workloads where frames arrive faster than they can be shown.
Has dedicated tests covering buffer handoff behavior.

pain: Locks in a hot single-producer or single-consumer path add latency for no good reason.
Contention inside the render pipeline shows up as dropped responsiveness.
subfeatures: Uses a lock-free single-producer or single-consumer queue for hot-path communication.
Avoids mutex overhead where one producer and one consumer are enough.
Improves predictability under sustained output.
Complements the triple-buffered rendering architecture rather than duplicating it.

pain: Terminal apps assume real escape-sequence compatibility and break on half-implemented emulators.
Developers should not have to debug the terminal instead of their tool.
subfeatures: Emulates core VT100 and xterm behavior expected by modern CLI tools.
Sets a standard xterm-256color environment for broad compatibility.
Encodes keyboard modifiers and special keys in terminal-appropriate sequences.
Keeps shell apps, TUIs, and remote sessions behaving like they expect.

pain: Broken wide-character handling turns modern CLI output into misaligned garbage.
AI and developer tools increasingly use Unicode, icons, and emoji in normal output.
subfeatures: Handles Unicode and wide glyph rendering in the terminal grid.
Preserves alignment for emoji and other multi-cell characters.
Lets modern CLI output render as intended instead of collapsing spacing.
Works with font customization so supported glyphs remain readable.

pain: Without shell hooks, a terminal cannot reliably know cwd, exit status, or prompt boundaries.
Features like context-aware titles and automation depend on real shell lifecycle data.
subfeatures: Creates integration scripts for Bash, Zsh, and Fish automatically.
Emits OSC 7 directory updates and exit-status signals back to Chau7.
Supplies CHAU7_SESSION_ID and CHAU7_TAB_ID so hooks know terminal context.
Runs at startup rather than as visible commands in shell history.

pain: Losing output history makes debugging, replay, and AI review much harder than it should be.
Fixed shallow buffers force you to choose between performance and remembering what happened.
subfeatures: Configurable scrollback line limits let you tune retention versus memory use.
Supports deep history tracking instead of only the visible screen.
Connects to line timestamp tracking and session capture features.
Can be cleared or exported through terminal APIs and UI flows.

pain: URLs and file references in terminal output should be actionable, not dead text.
Copy-paste from logs is slower and more error-prone than clicking.
subfeatures: Detects clickable links in terminal output so navigation is direct.
Supports terminal-native hyperlink semantics instead of only plain-text heuristics.
Keeps link handling inside the native terminal view rather than external overlays.
Makes build logs, docs links, and local paths faster to act on.

pain: Modern CLI tools output charts, screenshots, and previews that plain text cannot express.
Without graphics protocols, rich terminal tools fall back to worse UX.
subfeatures: Supports inline graphics protocols such as Sixel and Kitty graphics.
Provides settings-level control for image protocol behavior.
Renders images inside the terminal view instead of forcing an external preview.
Lets rich CLI tools stay rich inside Chau7.

pain: Developers who choose coding fonts expect their symbols to render properly.
Text appearance should be configurable per workflow, not fixed.
subfeatures: Optional ligature rendering for compatible fonts like Fira Code.
Integrated into appearance settings rather than requiring font hacks.
Lives beside font family and size controls so typography is coherent.
Persists through config and profile settings.

pain: Background commands, remote tasks, and long-running AI jobs finish silently if the bell is ignored.
One-size-fits-all bell behavior is either noisy or easy to miss.
subfeatures: Configurable bell enablement and selectable bell sounds.
Supports a visual bell alternative when sound is not desired.
Applies bell preferences directly to the terminal view layer.
Includes rate-limiting settings so noisy programs do not spam alerts.

pain: AI agents, pastes, and sleepy humans occasionally hit Enter on the wrong command.
Shells do not reliably warn before obviously destructive operations.
subfeatures: Detects risky command patterns before they are sent to the shell.
Presents a confirmation dialog instead of executing immediately.
Settings let you control guard behavior and highlight scope.
Uses the same terminal-aware command parsing path that avoids naive string matching.

pain: Closing the wrong tab can kill a deploy, SSH tunnel, or AI run instantly.
Generic tab close behavior is too aggressive for long-lived processes.
subfeatures: Warns before closing tabs with running processes.
Can be configured to warn only for active processes or for every close.
Uses native confirmation dialogs with context-aware messaging.
Covers close-tab, replace-tab, and close-window flows.

pain: Giving AI tools raw terminal control without review is fast right up until it is catastrophic.
Approval systems that do not understand context or agent identity create either noise or false trust.
subfeatures: Intercepts MCP tool calls before execution and shows the tool name plus parameters.
Supports Accept, Deny, Always Allow, and Audit Only outcomes.
Profiles can scope approvals by context and by allowed agent names.
Persisted allowlists let trusted commands stop prompting while new ones still pause.

pain: The system clipboard remembers only the last thing, not the five things you actually need.
Re-copying previous commands or snippets breaks flow.
subfeatures: Stores a rolling history of clipboard items inside Chau7.
Shows previews and metadata so you can pick the right item quickly.
Integrates with overlay controls for fast selection and paste-back.
Has dedicated tests covering storage and preview behavior.

pain: Multi-line pastes into shells are risky when the receiving app cannot distinguish pasted text from typing.
Paste safety depends on protocol support, and most users do not know when they have it.
subfeatures: Sends bracketed paste sequences when the running application requests them.
Falls back to raw paste when the app does not support the protocol.
Preserves direct paste semantics instead of inserting a fake preview layer.
Makes supporting shells and editors treat pasted newlines more safely.

pain: Repeated commands, bootstrap steps, and template blocks should not live in text files or memory.
Global aliases are too blunt when commands vary by repo or profile.
subfeatures: Snippets support user, profile, and repo scopes.
Placeholder and environment token parsing lets snippets stay dynamic.
Snippets are accessible from the overlay and command palette, not just a settings screen.
File monitoring and reload flows keep snippet sources current.

pain: Remembering hostnames, ports, usernames, keys, and bastions by hand is busywork.
Reconnecting to the same environments should be one action, not a mini-form every time.
subfeatures: Saved SSH connections capture host, port, username, identity file, and jump-host details.
Connection profiles are managed in a dedicated observable store instead of ad hoc shell aliases.
Launching uses proper shell escaping so saved settings survive odd values.
The manager backs both direct SSH workflows and imported SSH config entries.

pain: Existing ~/.ssh/config files should be assets, not migration projects.
Re-entering years of SSH host aliases into a terminal UI is needless friction.
subfeatures: Parses standard SSH config host entries into Chau7 connection data.
Supports serialization back from structured entries when needed.
Handles common fields like hostnames, users, ports, identities, and ProxyJump.
Keeps shared SSH profiles in sync with real config content instead of manual duplication.

pain: Bastion and ProxyJump flows are easy to mistype and annoying to remember.
Multi-hop SSH should not require rebuilding the full command every time.
subfeatures: Saved connections include a dedicated jump-host or ProxyJump field.
Parsing support preserves ProxyJump entries imported from SSH config.
Launch commands are shell-escaped correctly so jump hosts are reliable.
Works inside both hand-made connection profiles and imported config entries.

pain: A local project tab and a remote prod tab should not look or behave the same.
Manual profile switching is too easy to forget in context-heavy workflows.
subfeatures: Rules can match on directory, SSH host, and other session context.
Matching rules switch to the right saved profile automatically.
Chau7 remembers the previous profile and restores it when the context no longer matches.
Priority and named rules keep automated switching predictable instead of magical.

pain: Mouse-hunting for commands and settings wastes the benefit of a keyboard-first terminal.
Power features are useless if users cannot discover them quickly.
subfeatures: Fuzzy-searchable command palette similar to editor launchers.
Keyboard-first action execution keeps common operations one shortcut away.
Search state, clearing, and selection are handled inside the palette itself.
Palette actions can trigger operational commands like snippet reloads and other advanced flows.

pain: Searching long terminal output with scroll alone is primitive.
Losing the current match count or location makes output review slower than it should be.
subfeatures: Opens a dedicated search overlay on top of the active terminal.
Tracks query state, result lists, and match counts in the tab model.
Search highlights are rendered in the terminal overlay instead of mutating buffer text.
Closing search resets query and results cleanly.

pain: The commands you want are usually the ones you typed days ago and forgot.
Raw shell history is messy without filtering and structure.
subfeatures: Searches the persistent command history store instead of only the current buffer.
Returns commands with metadata such as timestamps for better recall.
Supports query and limit flows reused by scripting APIs.
Makes previously successful commands reusable without retyping.

pain: Readability and density vary wildly across developers and displays.
A terminal that locks you into one font setup slows you down every day.
subfeatures: Lets you choose font family and size instead of relying on a fixed stack.
Configuration is persisted through settings and config-file parsing.
Typography changes apply across the native app rather than through CSS-like hacks.
Plays well with ligatures, cursor styling, and editor settings.

pain: Color schemes affect legibility, focus, and error recognition constantly.
One default theme cannot suit day, night, presentations, and remote work equally well.
subfeatures: Supports multiple terminal color schemes and appearance presets.
Theme choices can live inside saved profiles for context-specific setups.
Integrates with tab colors and other appearance settings for a coherent look.
Settings infrastructure persists theme changes cleanly.

pain: Fixed shortcuts fight years of muscle memory from other terminals and editors.
Power users need the terminal to adapt to their workflow, not the reverse.
subfeatures: Keybindings map terminal and overlay actions to custom shortcuts.
The keybinding manager centralizes action names instead of scattering shortcuts in the UI.
Custom bindings can cover navigation, snippets, search, and window behaviors.
Shortcut labels stay human-readable through the same mapping layer.

pain: Cursor visibility and shape matter more than people admit, especially across editing modes.
A one-size cursor can feel wrong on high-density displays or mixed workflows.
subfeatures: Supports configurable cursor styles such as block, beam, or underline.
Cursor blink behavior is configurable alongside shape.
Cursor settings persist through config and saved preferences.
Changes integrate with the native renderer rather than a text-only workaround.

pain: Floating terminals should feel native on macOS, not like opaque rectangles pasted over your desktop.
Transparency without blur often hurts readability instead of helping it.
subfeatures: Overlay appearance supports adjustable opacity for the terminal window.
Blur and material-style compositing keep content readable over busy backgrounds.
Works naturally with the dedicated overlay window mode.
Appearance settings let you tune atmosphere without sacrificing text contrast.

pain: Text jammed against the terminal edge is harder to scan and uglier than it needs to be.
Different workflows need different density and whitespace tradeoffs.
subfeatures: Lets you control terminal padding and visual breathing room.
Layout changes apply within the native terminal view, not by hacking prompt text.
Spacing can be combined with fonts and themes inside profiles.
Helps presentation-style and daily-driver setups coexist.

pain: Some workflows need a terminal that floats over everything without behaving like a normal app window.
Switching desktops or rearranging windows to reach a shell is needless overhead.
subfeatures: Runs Chau7 in a dedicated floating overlay window.
Supports transparency and blur as first-class overlay behaviors.
Remembers position and visual state for workspace-oriented use.
Keeps the full tab model inside the overlay rather than reducing it to a scratch console.

pain: The fastest terminal is the one you can summon and dismiss instantly.
Traditional window activation is slower than a global hotkey for quick terminal checks.
subfeatures: Supports a Quake-style dropdown terminal from the top of the screen.
Global hotkey toggles visibility without manual window management.
Height and animation behavior are configurable.
Can coexist with the menu bar status item and other window modes.

pain: Focused sessions sometimes need all available space and fewer distractions.
Pseudo-fullscreen workarounds do not integrate cleanly with macOS Spaces.
subfeatures: Uses native macOS fullscreen behavior instead of a fake maximized window.
Integrates with menu bar hiding and standard Space transitions.
Fullscreen behavior is wired into the app's native window controllers.
Can be toggled like a normal window mode without losing terminal state.

pain: Some users want Chau7 always available but never cluttering the Dock or app switcher.
A resident terminal utility needs a proper background access point, not a hidden window gamble.
subfeatures: Keeps a persistent NSStatusItem in the macOS menu bar.
Uses a long-lived NSPopover command center instead of recreating panel state on every open.
Switching to accessory activation hides Chau7 from the Dock and Cmd+Tab.
Terminal sessions continue running even when the main window is hidden.

pain: Editing scripts or config without syntax cues is an easy way to miss mistakes.
Terminals that embed editing but skip highlighting stop being useful the moment code appears.
subfeatures: Uses a dedicated syntax highlighter inside Chau7 editor views.
Language metadata helps colorize different file types appropriately.
Highlighting is integrated with the editor instead of bolted on as static rich text.
Works alongside find, brackets, and line numbers for a fuller editing experience.

pain: Nested shells, configs, and code blocks are hard to read when delimiters are visually anonymous.
Missing one bracket can waste more time than this feature costs to implement.
subfeatures: Editor logic recognizes matching pairs for common bracket characters.
Matching can be highlighted as you move the cursor.
The behavior is configurable through editor settings.
It complements syntax highlighting rather than competing with it.

pain: Inline editing without search and replace forces repetitive manual fixes.
A terminal editor should not stop at cursor movement and raw text entry.
subfeatures: Built-in find and replace tools reduce repetitive editing work.
Search integrates with the editor view rather than external dialogs only.
Complements syntax highlighting and line numbers for real editing workflows.
Keeps quick config and script edits inside Chau7 instead of bouncing to another editor.

pain: Code, stack traces, and review comments depend on stable line references.
Editing without line numbers makes navigation and communication needlessly vague.
subfeatures: Displays line numbers alongside editor content.
Stays aligned with the scrolling editor view.
Works with search and bracket matching for faster navigation.
Makes quick terminal-side edits easier to discuss and verify.

pain: Accessibility fails fast when controls are unlabeled or focus order is inconsistent.
A terminal UI with overlays, tabs, and agent states needs explicit screen-reader support.
subfeatures: Adds accessibility labels and semantic descriptions across key UI elements.
Keeps interactive overlays navigable for assistive technologies.
Applies accessibility work to AI-specific chrome, not just generic buttons.
The codebase includes dedicated accessibility testing around these flows.

pain: Low-contrast terminal chrome and overlays are hard to use in bright or visually demanding environments.
Accessibility is not solved by a theme if critical states are still visually subtle.
subfeatures: Emphasizes stronger text, borders, and state contrast for readability.
Makes overlays, badges, and controls easier to distinguish at a glance.
Lives within the native appearance system rather than a separate web-style skin.
Supports accessibility-minded setups alongside fonts, themes, and reduced motion.

pain: Motion-heavy UI can be distracting or inaccessible, especially in transient overlays.
Window and tab animations should respect system accessibility preferences.
subfeatures: Checks reduce-motion preferences before running nonessential animations.
Tones down overlay and transition effects when motion should be minimized.
Keeps the interface readable and predictable without flashy movement.
Applies across UI flows where Chau7 would otherwise animate state changes.

pain: Developer tools are too often English-only, which turns simple settings into friction for global teams.
Localization is hard to retrofit unless strings and resources are structured from the start.
subfeatures: Uses localized string bundles and a dedicated localization layer.
Ships multiple lproj resources instead of hardcoding copy in views.
Includes tests for the localization infrastructure.
Makes UI, settings, and feature labels translatable across the app.

pain: AppleScript, UI scripting, and simulated keypresses are the wrong abstraction for terminal automation.
A real local API should work from any language without vendor lock-in.
subfeatures: Exposes a Unix-socket JSON-RPC interface at ~/Library/Application Support/Chau7/scripting.sock.
Supports methods for history, settings, tabs, command execution, and snippets.
Uses structured request routing and standard error handling instead of fragile text commands.
Is backed by dedicated scripting API tests.

pain: Automation and context awareness break down when the terminal cannot observe command completion or directory changes.
Manual hook plumbing across shells is tedious unless the terminal owns the integration.
subfeatures: Provides post-command hooks for exit-code reporting and directory-change hooks for cwd tracking.
Supports Bash, Zsh, and Fish with generated integration scripts.
Exposes CHAU7_SESSION_ID and CHAU7_TAB_ID so hooks know where they are reporting.
Uses escape-sequence signaling so the terminal can react without parsing prompts.
